bullets.text: Underwriting profit margin collapsed from 8.4% in 1998 to 1.7% in 1999 in Personal Lines, driven by a sudden reversal in loss frequency trends; EPS fell from $6.11 to $3.96 and the stock plummeted from a high of $174+ to $73.125 at year-end.
Personal Lines net premiums written grew 16% to $5,702.4 million; Progressive became the 4th largest U.S. auto insurer; five-year compound annual growth rate from 1994 to 1999 was 20%.

Progressive cut rates 1.3% in the first half of 1999, then reversed course with 4.4% rate increases in the second half; reverted to calendar-year 96 combined ratio target, abandoning the lifetime-of-policyholder profit approach adopted in 1996.

Unexpected reversal in long-term accident frequency and severity improvement drove unanticipated loss cost increases; reserve releases fell to 0.5 combined ratio points versus 3.7 in 1998, 2.5 in 1997, and 3.3 in 1996.

With 7,561 claim representatives averaging only 3.7 years tenure (up from 3,093 in 1994), claim handling quality was pressured; in-person claim contact within nine hours achieved 42% of the time; five 24/7 call centers and 350 field offices operational.

Progressive sold more than $70 million in auto insurance on the Internet in 1999 (7% of direct sales); gave over 1 million online quotes and serviced 65,000 customers online; offered buying in 47 states plus D.C.

Progressive completed multi-year rollout of direct distribution advertising to all markets; adjusted advertising agencies and campaigns in response to Super Bowl XXXIII spending that generated awareness but not proportionate sales.

Issued $300 million of 30-year senior debt at 6.625% to refinance $300 million maturing at year-end 2000; debt-to-equity ratio was 27.6%; no open-market share repurchases in 1999; common stock investments grew to $1,243.6 million (19.3% of assets).

Glenn Renwick officially became CEO-Insurance Operations on January 1, 2000, completing Progressive's generational management transition; Lewis noted this as a key accomplishment despite the difficult operating year.

Auto insurance entered a cyclical downturn in 1999 after 1992-1998 industry loss ratios near break-even; Progressive acknowledged direct competition with all major auto insurers including State Farm, Allstate, and GEICO.
